APPELLANT ; DEFENDANT, AND TEHMERT ; : . . RESPONDENT. INFORMANT, ON APPEAL FROM COURT OF GENERAL SESSIONS OF THE STATE OF VICTORIA.

The Constitution (63 : 64 Viet., c. 12), secs. 71, 73, 1903 (Vo. 6 of 1903), secs. 30, 38, 34, 39, 63, 7 (No.1105), se. 127—A uthority of Parliament to confer appellate federal juriedi tion on other Court than High Court—Whether appellate jurisdiction conferred om State Courts— Offence against Commonwealth Law—Summary Conviction— Appeal to State Court—Remedy where Court denies jurisdiction— Mandamus gyipth 63, and Appeal, Barton d. 5, 16, 77—The Judiciary Act H.C. or A, 80—Juatices Act 1890( Victoria) 1905. j Mexnovn August 2, 3, 7. Where a Court of a State declines jurisdiction in a matter as to which it is invested with federal jurisdiction, the remedy is by recourse to the appellate Jutisdiction of the High Court. 'The federal jurisdiction which Parliament is by sec. 77 of the Constitution suthorized to confer upon the Courts of the several States, and upon federal Gourts other than the High Court, includes both original and appellate Jotisdiction, 'Leuuerr. HIGH COURT See. 39 of the Judiciary Act 1903 is a valid exercise of the « conferred, and under it the Courts of the several States havefederlan jurisdiction, as regards the matters enumerated in secs, 75 aul eae Constitution, to the same extent that, and subject to the same o under the State laws they have appellate jurisilietion im matters to wha State laws apply.

Held, therefore, that © person being convicted in Vietoria by Magistrate of an offence under see. 7 of the Zmmigration Restriction Au iy and by see. 127 of the Justices Act 1890 (Vict.), an appeal lying f conviction by a Police Magistrate to a Court of General Session, sud may appeal to a Court of General Sessions, Manpamus. Before James Anderson Panton, Esq., a Police Magistrate, 'Ah Vick was, on the information of Albert Lehmert, charged and being convicted, was ordered to be imprisoned for fom days. The defendant gave notice of appeal against this conve tion to the Court of General Sessions under the provisions of the Justices Act 1890 (Victoria).

The appeal coming on for h before His Honor Judge Johnston, Chairman of the Court of General Sessions, objection was taken that the Court had m jurisdiction to entertain the appeal, inasmuch as the matter being. one of federal jurisdiction, an appeal could only be had to High Court. On this ground the learned Judge held that he had no jurisdiction. An order nisi was then obtained from th High Court calling upon the learned Judge and the informant show cause why a writ of mandamus should not issue to compl the learned Judge to hear and determine the appeal. : Coldham (with him Ah Ket), for the defendant, moved theo der absolute. Harrison Moore, for His Honor Judge Johnston. 'The jurist tion under which the Police Magistrate acted is{eonferred by 68 of the Judiciary Act 1903.

Two questions arise, whether any right of appeal was given by that Act, and if so, hed t Court of General Sessions jurisdiction to entertain the ah " See. 68 deals exclusively with original jurisdiction, and not mi appellate jurisdiction, Sub-see. (3) of that section is definite ":0LR] OF AUSTRALIA. the only person who can deal with such a matter as the present, jsone of the persons therein described. Sec. 72 of the Judiciary '4e,1903 deals with appeals in cases of indictment, but if sec. 68 gives an appellate jurisdiction, it would by sub-see. (2) (c) bring jn the appellate jurisdiction of the State Courts as to indictments, "ngite of sec. 72. By sec. 4 of the Punishment of Offences Act 1W0l, which was passed as a temporary measure until the tstablishment of the High Court, an appellate jurisdiction was given to the Courts of the States in such a matter as the present.

Itis impossible to suppose that the legislature intended the same resilt to follow from the totally different provisions of the Judiciary Act 1903, If sec. 68 has nothing to do with appeals, tie appellate jurisdiction of the Court of General Sessions and theright to appeal must be sought elsewhere. Sec. 39 of the Judiciary Act 1903 confers certain federal jurisdiction on the eyeral State Courts. It is in a Part of the Act distinct from that jnwhich see: 68 is.

Part X.,in whieh see, 68 occurs, deals throughout with matters which are made offences by the laws of the Commonwealth, and deals with them exclusively and exhaustively. Sec.39 deals with civil jurisdiction, and with certain criminal jurisdiction in matters as to which the State Courts would ordinarily have jurisdiction, but in which some federal matter intervenes which transfers the case to federal jurisdiction. The present offence iswholly a creature of federal Statute, and any jurisdiction as to itis given by sec. 68 and not by sec. 39. If sec. 39 did include "tise like the present, it might bring into operation the whole of the State laws respecting appeals in cases of indictment, although the appellate jurisdiction in criminal matters is dealt with tshaustively by sec. 72.

There is no doubt that the appellate jurisdiction eontended for in the present case is within the words _ofsee, 39, but a consideration of other parts of the Judiciary -Attshows that that section is not to have such a wide meaning. _ S39 merely deals with the transformation of State jurisdiction _iitofederal jurisdiction ; if it was intended to have the wider Meaning contended for, sec. 68 would be superfluous. Even if 39 gives this appellate jurisdiction, no right of appeal is given Wit See. 4 of the Punishment of Offences Act 1901 did give 4: point, using emphatic negative words, and providing that W.C.or a. 1905, , Au Yiok Lunstens. See, 89 confer oriag jurisdiction only, and not appellate jurisdiction, Tt is one af « set of sections dealing with original jurisdiction only, the applay jurisdiction of the High Court being dealt with elsewhe: Assuming that see. 39 is effective to incorporate the appl provisions of the State laws and also the provisions of the laws giving the right of appeal—in this case see 127 of the Justices Act 1890 (Vict.)—the penalty imposed by see. 7 of the Immigration Restriction Act 1901 is of such a peculiar nator that it does not fall within see. 127 of the former Act. It ismo nearly like the penalty referred to in the proviso to that seetin As to the power of this Court to issue a prerogative writ of mn damus, it may be that under sec. 75 (v.) of the Constitution that power only exists where the writ is sought against an offer o the Commonwealth. [He also referred to Curtis's Jurisdictig of Courts of United States, pp. 67, 68.] Cussen (with him Power), for the respondent Lehmert. An appellate jurisdiction is not conferred on a Court of Gene Sessions by sec. 39 of the Judiciary Act 1903, and if it is pur ported to be conferred, Parliament has gone beyond its powers under the Constitution.

The Customs Act 1901, the Bacise Ad 1901, and the Distillation Act 1901, all contained special provi sions for appeals to the Courts of the States. Then the Pun ment of Offences Act 1901 by sec. 4 gave an appeal to the Courts of the States as to offences against laws of the Commonwealth at a time when Parliament was contemplating the ereation ofthe High Court. It is a reasonable inference from prior legislation and from the terms of the Judiciary Act 1903, that the intention of the legislation was that the High Court should be the only Court of Appeal for offences against the Commonwealth I That inference should be drawn even though the terms of see. 39 (2) of the Judiciary Act 1903 when read by themselte appear to give an appellate jurisdiction to the Courts of States. Secs. 68 to 72 of the Judiciary Act 1903 support this (1) 2 T.R., 604, CLR] OF AUSTRALIA. forming a group of sections apart from sec. 73, which with appellate jurisdiction, no authority is by sec, 77 ven to Parliament to invest the Courts of the States, or "the Courts created by Parliament, with appellate jurisdiction, The use of the words " with respect toany of the matters mentioned in the last two sections," ie. with respect to the matters javhich the High Court is given original jurisdiction, shows that 'deily, The meaning of those words is that Parliament may 'Courts with power, to do what the High Court can do under secs. It says tat the judicial power of the Commonwealth, which must include ypellate as well as original jurisdiction, shall be vested in the High Court and "in such federal Courts as the Parliament es and in such other Courts as it invests with federal jurision."} 'That section must be controlled by the subsequent sections. 8.71 only deals with the creation of Courts other than the ligh Court, while sec. 77 provides for the matters with which those Courts are to deal.

The Judiciary Act 1903 carries out view. Part IV. deals with original jurisdiction of the High 'Court, Part V. with its appellate jurisdiction, and Part VI. deals th exclusive and with invested jurisdiction, The exclusive 'jistiction must be original jurisdiction only, and invested jurislition was also intended to be original jurisdiction only, In Quick ¢ Garran's Australian Constitution, at p. 802, the view taken that the invested jurisdiction includes both original and 'gpellate jurisdiction, and Martin v. 'The construction put the respondent upon sec. 77 of the Constitution is too narrow. requires the insertion of the word "original" before the word {misdietion" wherever it ovours in that section, Where it was tended to distinguish between appellate and original jurisdiction ithas been done in specific language. The judicial power of the monwealth—and that includes appellate as well as original wwer—is by sec. 71 vested not only in the High Court, but also such other Courts as Parliament creates, and such other Courts 1sParliament invests with federal jurisdiction. If the respondent's jew is correct, Parliament cannot invest either State Courts, or federal Courts of its own creation, with appellate jurisdiction. iy see, 71 of the Constitution nothing is created or done; the sation is merely matter declaratory. Sec. 51 (xxxix.) gives Parlament authority to make laws as to matters incidental to the exeeution of any power vested by the Constitution in the federal jndieature, 'The determination as to which of the Courts created ly Parliament, or invested by it with federal jurisdiction, shall exercise original jurisdiction, and which appellate jurisdiction, is matter incidental to the execution of a power vested by the Constitution in the federal judicature.

Sec. 77 was intended to over the difficulties which arose in the United States. The Parliament has effectively given an appellate jurisdiction in this matter to the Court of General Sessions of Victoria. The words "feleral jurisdiction" in see. 39 of the Judiciary Act 1903 are in the same sense as they are used in sec. 7 of the Constitu'tion.

The intention of Parliament was that the several Courts of the States should hear and determine matters as to which they fre given federal jurisdiction in the same way as they would har and determine matters as to which, as State Courts, they had Itisdiction. The only conclusion drawn from Part V. of the Judiciary Act 1903 is in the ap lant's favour.

A jurisdiction to hear the appeal is conferred this ease on the Court of General Sessions. 'The argument th there is a presumption that no right of appeal exists unless it given in express terms has been pushed too far, » Whether the appellant has gone to the right Court or not, is a person who has a right to appeal: The Constitution, ee 7 If he is such a person, why should he not go to the Court has jurisdiction to entertain this particular class of appeals? Parliament intended by the Judiciary Act 1903 to do away the right of appeals to the State Courts in federal matters, Customs Act 1901, the Beer Excise Act 1901, the Distillation 1901, and the Excise Act 1901, which give a right of app certain matters to the State Courts. By sec. 79 of the Judiciary Act 1903 it was intended not only that the laws of procedi each State, but also those laws giving substantive rights, sh apply to Courts exercising federal jurisdiction. 'The nature of th penalty imposed by sec. 7 of the Immigration Restriction Ad 1901 does not bring the case within the proviso to see. 127 of t Justices Act 1890 (Viet.).

The imprisonment is absolute in' first instance, and there is no order to find security. Mandama is the proper remedy in this case. Sec. 80 of the Judiciary 4 1903, which directs that, so far as the provisions of the laws 0 the Commonwealth are inadequate to provide adequate reme! the common law of England shall govern all Courts € federal jurisdiction, was intended for this very class of OF AUSTRALIA. appellate jurisdiction of this Court.

The granting of writs of juandamus, prohibition, and habeas corpus at common law may be iction, and jn another as the exercise of appellate jurisdiction. When jere is a general appeal from an inferior Court to another Court, Court of Appeal can entertain any matter, however arising, shich shows that the decision of the Court appealed from is 'enoneous. The error may consist in a wrong determination of a y before the Court for its decision, or it may consis in an assertion by that Court of a jurisdiction which it does 'not possess, or it may consist in a refusal of that Court to exercise ajurisdiction which it possesses.

In all these cases the Court of Appeal ean exer For instanee, if the Court of Appeal in England appeal from the King's Bench Division in a case in which no appeal lay, the remedy would be by appeal to the House of Lords, and that tribunal, as it has, I think, done in some e its appellate jurisdiction in order to set the instances, would allow the appeal and reverse the decision of the Court of Appeal, on the ground that it had sought to exercise a utisdiction which it did not possess. In the same way if the Court of Appeal declined to entertain an appeal from the King's Bench Division in a case in which it could entertain an appeal, the House of Lords, as an appellate tribunal, would set it right. lese considerations show that this is really an invocation of the ppellate jurisdiction of this Court. It was so held in the United States in 1803 in Marbury v.

Madison (1). No question therefore arises as to the validity of sec. 33 of the Judiciary Act 1903, which provides that "The High Court may make orders or direct, the issue of writs—(«) commanding the performance by any Court invested with federal jurisdiction, of any duty relating to Neexercise of its federal jurisdiction ; or (b) requiring any Court abstain from the exercise of any federal jurisdiction which it toes not possess; or . . . Lehmert, federal jurisdiction conferred upon them by Statute, It is m seriously in controversy that, if the offence charged in the before us had been an offence created by State law, an appa would lie to the Court of General Sessions. By that law ax appeal to the Court of General Sessions is given from a convietim of a Court of Petty Sessions imposing a penalty exceeding & or a term of imprisonment, with a proviso that an appeal do not lie where imprisonment is adjudged for failure to eompl with an order for the finding of sureties or for the giving of aay security. The appellant in the present case was sentenced term of imprisonment.

A point was made that, owing to peculiar language of sec. 7 of the Immigration Restriction A¢ 1901, an appeal might not lie from a conviction under it Court of General Sessions. However, the argument was seriously pressed, and I do not think there is anything init Whether the Court of General Sessions had jurisdietia and of the Judiciary Act 1903. The Constitution (see. a provides that: "The judicial power of the Commonwealth sh be vested in a Federal Supreme Court, to be called the H Court of Australia, and in such other federal Courts as the P R] OF AUSTRALIA. ribute of sovereignty which must of necessity be exercised by 'ene tribunal, that that tribunal must be constituted by the 'overeign power, and that the limits within which the judicial 'gar is to be exereised by the tribunal must be defined.

In the "ase of the High Court, the extent to which that Court may exerse judicial power is defined by the Constitution; in the ease of "ther Courts it is not defined by the Constitution, and must, again "i{neeessity, be defined by the Commonwealth law which creates "those Courts or invests them with federal jurisdiction. 'The term "Sfederal jurisdiction " means authority to exercise the judicial "over of the Commonwealth, and again that must be within limits "prescribed. 'Then "federal jurisdiction" must include appellate _ jurisdiction as well as original juri; diction, The whole scheme of the Constitution assumes that the judicial power includes both in the ease of the High Court, and from the history of the Constitutio and the practice in English-speaking countries, it must be taken for granted that the judicial power was known by the "framers of the Constitution to include both, and that those framers intended that the judicial power might be exercised by Courts of triginal jurisdiction or by Courts of appellate jurisdiction. Then ste, 73 of the Constitution defines the appellate jurisdiction of the High Court. Amongst other matters of appellate jurisdiction the "High Court is authorized to hear appeals from all Courts having _ federal jurisdiction, "with such exceptions and subject to such _ Ngulations as the Parliament prescribes," and none have been pretibed which affect the present case. Sec. 75 defines and enumer_ ates five classes of cases in which the High Court has original jmisdiction, and sec. 76 four others in which Parliament may confer original jurisdiction upon the High Court.

In all other huatters, as at present advised, I think the High Court has no original jurisdiction, and cannot, gud High Court, have it. Then _ "t. 77 provides that Parliament may make laws—"(1.) Defining the jurisdiction of any federal Court other than the High Court," td "(111 Investing any Court of a State with federal jurisdiction" Now, the power to ereate a federal Court depends upon vatition." HIGH COURT ment to say what jurisdiction each Court shall have, ty see. 71 into consideration, see. 77 (1.) means that the Pars may establish any Court to be called a federal Court, and give it jurisdiction to exercise any judicial power of the Common wealth, which the Parliament may think fit to confer unm either by way of appellate or original jurisdiction, Subse must receive a precisely similar interpretation, Parliament invest any Court of a State with authority to exercise fede judicial power, again to the extent prescribed by the Sta There is nothing to restrict that judicial power to original diction any more than to appellate jurisdiction, and there reason why there should be a restriction. 'There can be no doth that Parliament might think fit to invest one Court exelusvdy with original jurisdiction, another with appellate jurisditon and another with both. There is nothing to limit that y be conferred on any Court which Parliament thinks fit invest with federal jurisdiction.

Those being the powers of Parliament, we come next to sec 3 exercise its powers, and the question is, what is the effeet of th enactment? There is no doubt that sec. 39 is framed for th dictions, whether such limits are as to locality, subject-matter, t otherwise, be invested with federal jurisdiction, in all matters whieh the High Court has original jurisdiction, or in which on inal jurisdiction ean be conferred upon it, exeept as provide the last preceding section, and subject to the following eon words "in all matters in which the High Court has original j % diction, or in whieh original jurisdiction ean be conferred uponit, are mere words of reference to the matters enumerated in sees 7? and 76 of the Constitution. With respect to those matters, those only, the several Courts are invested with federal Nair tion. The circumstance that the words " original Jurisdiction used twice in see. 39 of the Judiciary Act does not in any OF AUSTRALIA. ence, We start then with this—as to the nine classes of cases enumerated in secs. 75 and 76 of the Constitution the several Courts of the States are invested with federal jurisdiction.

I have already pointed out what "federal jurisdiction" means. But the _jarisdietion with which those Courts are invested is qualified by hewords " within the limits of their several jurisdictions" The 'mactment is general and applies to all the Courts of all the States. Some of the Courts of the States have original jurisdiction only. 'Two at least of those Courts have appellate jurisdiction only.

A geat many have both original and appellate jurisdiction. 'This 'ment relates to all the Courts of the States, and may be read ifall those Courts were enumerated. Let us take the Court of General Sessions of Victoria and apply the section to this Court, and it will read thus :—* Courts of General Sessions of the State {Victoria shall within the limits of their jurisdiction be invested ith federal jurisdiction in all matters enumerated in secs. 75 and 6 of the Constitution." Then we have to inquire what is the jurisdistion of Courts of General Sessions in Victoria? On that inquiry we find that they have both original and appellate jurisdiction. hy should it be said, when those Courts can exercise the judicial power of the Commonwealth within the limits of theirjurisdietion, that it is to be limited to original jurisdiction, and is not to include appellate jurisdiction ? I can see no sufficient reason for so limit agthewords, In my judgment, sec. 39 confers authority on each e Court to exereise the judicial power of the Commonwealth 'inthe enumerated classes of cases, as to all such matters as are nother respects within the limits of its jurisdiction as defined by the State laws by which it is established, And I think that authority is conferred to the same extent, and for the same oses, and is to be exercised in the same manner, as if the Court had been established as a federal Court with jurisdiction to 'ereise the federal judicial power to the extent, and for the poses, for which it was actually established. That being so, 'tappears to me that, on the plain words of see. 39, the Court of General Sessions had authority to exereise its appellate jurisdieom, and to hear the appeal from a Police Magistrate, with regard 'Wanoffence against the Zmmiyration Restriction Act 1901.

That Jinit the subject of the power—the words are mere words of referH.C. oF A. 1905. Au Yiox Lemire, Gritith C3. it — An Yrex 'Urner, Grifin C3. H.0.0r A. appears to me the literal construction of see. 89, an ii HIGH COURT sible to arrive at any other eonelusion without impaing, upon the words which is not to be found within the setin Relianee, however, has been placed upon previons lesan Act called the Punishment of Offences Act 1901, passed inD 1901, and which was a temporary Act, contained a provision sec. 2 that:—*The law in each State r specting the arrest a custody of offenders, and the procedure for their summary ci vietion or for their examination and commitment for til q indictment or information and for holding aceused 'persons to hai shall apply and be applied so far as they are applicable sons who are charged with offences against the laws of the Commonwealth committed within the State." It further pr vided by sec. 3 that the several Courts and magistrates of State, exercising jurisdiction as to the matters mentioned i should have the like jurisdiction with respect to persons charg with offences against the laws of the Commonwealth, with apm trate of the State who is specially authorized by the Govern General to exercise such jurisdiction." See. 4 of the Actexpr gave an appeal from any Court of a State exercising juris under the Act "to the Court and in the manner provided by th law of that State for appeal from the like convictions jug sentences or orders in respect of persons changed with oft against the laws of that State." 'That Act was a temporary to cease to have effect npon the establishment of the High Gm It was therefore conferring federal jurisdiction of a certain dis upon Courts of the States, and, as by the Constitution a appeal from the decisions of such Courts lay to the High Court, there being no High Court then constituted, result would have been that, if see. 4 had not existed, would have been no practicable appeal—it would have been appeal in name, but toa non-existent Court. Tt was ne therefore, to make provision for some appeal, and it was natural that that provision should be temporary, becaus a8 as the High Court was established, it beeame an appellate 0 RJ OF AUSTRALIA. tion of the High Court were contemplated and proposed before per 1901. When the Judiciary Act came to be passed in 903, it was no longer necessary to provide special Courts of ppeal from summary convictions by State Courts.

Whether + Courts of Appeal should be created or not was a matter for consideration of Parliament. It might have been contented toleave appeals to the High Court, or it might have created other termediate appeal Courts. TE the construction of sec. 39 of the Judiciary Act 1903 which have given be correct, the Parliament has adopted the latter course, and has conferred upon State Courts which have appellate jurisdiction similar appellate federal jurisdiction in the enumerated asses of cases.

So that no inference can be drawn from the "aistence of the appellate jurisdiction created by the Punishment "of Offenders Act 1901, and the fact that the language creating "that jurisdiction is not repeated in terms in the Judiciary Act 1003. ~ Astronger argument was drawn from see. 68 of the Judiciary et 1903, whieh, in effect, repeats the provisions of sees. 2 and Sof the Punishment of Offenders Act 1901, but does not repeat those of sec, 4 of that Act. Now, if secs. 68 and 39 of the Judiciary Act 1903 covered precisely the same ground, there might be some force in that argument, though it would still be entrary to the accepted canons of construction to hold that, where there are two affirmative enactments in the same Act each dealing with the same matter, one is to be taken as negativing the other. Bit on examination it will be seen that secs. 68 and 39 probably donot cover the same ground.

See. 39 applies only to the nine tdhsses of cases enumerated in sees. 75 and 76 of the Constitution. Ste. 68 applies to all persons charged with offences against the laws of the Commonwealth. Now, unless it ean be asserted that there can be no offence against the laws of the Commonwealth Thich does not fall within one of the nine classes of cases eiumerated in secs. 75 and 76 of the Constitution, see. 68 of the Tuticiary Act 1903 was necessary. I should be very sorry to 'firm that secs. 75 and 76 of the Constitution do cover every ible case of offences against the laws of the Commonwealth. all these cases.
Now, the Judiciary Act 1903 and the eonstiH.C. or A. 1905, —S Au Yrox v Lesttenr. Griith C3. H.C. or A. 'They cover every offence against the Statutes of the Com a, Au Yiex Learner.
Grittin Cs. HIGH COURT whether those sections cover them. At any rate that poin not so clear as not to admit of argument.

In that view se jurisdiction of the High Court. For these reasons I am of opinion that the Court of Gen Sessions had jurisdiction to hear and determine the appeal fm the Police Magistrate, and ought to have entertained it therefore that this appeal should be allowed. Barron J.

Iam of the same opinion. His Honor the (hi Justice has said that this is a case of the exercise of ap rather than of original jurisdiction. While agreeing with Ido not for a moment say that this is not a case in which mandamus could issue from this Court.

I am not sure it i actually necessary to make the special provision contained in see 33 of the Judiciary Act 1903 authorizing the issue of writed mandamus, It is established in the case of Marbury v. Madi (1), that, if a mandamus is sought from a Court of appellat jurisdiction, it must be shown that its grant is an exercsé 0 appellate jurisdiction, The position is clearly stated in Quid and Garian's Constitution of the Australian Commonwealth at p. 779. It is as follows:—"The principles established in Marhin y.

Mudison are very clear. Where a writ of mandamus is sough the first question is whether 'the principles and usages of law' warrant the issue of a mandamus as the proper remedy in the _ and if that question is answered in the affirmative, the questa remains whether the Supreme Court has jurisdiction over Ha parties or the subject-matter. If the mandamus is sought agua a non-judieial officer, it is an exercise of original jurisietive and the Court can only act if the matter comes within scope of its original jurisdiction." (That is the manda provided for by sub-see. (v.) of see, 75 of the Australian stitution.) "If the mandamus is sought against @ Court it (1) 1 Cranch,, 137.

OLR] OF AUSTRALIA. {the matter comes within the scope of its appellate jurisdiction." represents the law as laid down in Marbury v. Madison (1). Ofeouse it is well known that there is a difference between our Constitution and that of the United States, because in the "former, original jurisdiction is by see. 75 (v.) given to the High (Court in matters in which mandamus is sought against a nonjuiiloffcer of the Commonwealth. 'Thatease was not provided forin the United States Constitution, and hence the decision in 'Morhury v.
